narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your second-decile ranking in Year 1 and Year 3 grades at school—both notably lower than the typical student's fifth- or sixth-decile standing—along with your LSAT score of 28, which falls well below the average of around 37. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 is actually a bit stronger than the typical 3.21, your lower-middle family income bracket is somewhat below average, and as a full-time White female applicant, your demographics align closely with the majority of students in the dataset. When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 decile rank emerged as the single most influential factor, strongly nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar failure due to its low standing relative to peers. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which had the next biggest pull in the same direction because it's significantly lower than what most successful students achieve. Your Year 1 decile rank came third in influence, again pushing toward failure for similar reasons of underperformance compared to the norm. Family income followed as the fourth-most important, with your lower-middle status contributing to the risk assessment in a way that typical higher brackets do not. Even your solid UGPA, while helpful in general, ranked fifth and still tilted the scales slightly toward predicting struggle on the bar. Less influential but still factoring in were your racial background as White—which is common and neutral here—and your full-time status, with gender having the smallest impact overall. In essence, your early academic deciles and LSAT stood out as the biggest drags compared to typical admitted students who pass the bar. To boost your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 37 or higher, strengthen your academic performance to climb those school deciles through dedicated study habits, and consider building a stronger overall GPA if possible before reapplying. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas, so with targeted effort, you could shift the model's view in your favor. Keep pushing—you've already shown undergraduate strength that bodes well for improvement.
